question_data.passage:
=== Document 1: Memo ===

From: HR Department
To: All Employees
Subject: Mandatory Cybersecurity Training
Date: October 5

All employees must complete the new cybersecurity training module by November 1. The training takes approximately 90 minutes and can be accessed through the company learning portal. Failure to complete by the deadline may result in temporary suspension of system access.

=== Document 2: Email ===

From: maria.lopez@company.com
To: hr@company.com
Subject: Training Extension Request

Hello,

I'm currently on a business trip in Europe until November 8 and won't have reliable access to complete the training before November 1. Could I receive an extension until November 15? I'll complete it immediately upon my return.

Thanks,
Maria

questions.stem: What is the standard deadline?
questions.explanation: Document 1「by November 1」が根拠。

questions.stem: Why does Maria need an extension?
choices: Business trip in Europe until November 8
questions.explanation: Document 2「I'm currently on a business trip in Europe until November 8」が根拠。

question_data.audio_script:
[IELTS Listening Section 2 - Bookstore Event Announcement]

Good evening, and welcome to Riverside Books. I'm Michael, the events coordinator, and I'd like to announce some exciting upcoming events.

This Saturday, we have author Helen Graham reading from her new novel 'The Last Light.' The reading begins at 3 PM, with a Q&A and book signing afterward. The event is free, but seating is limited to 50, so please reserve your spot in advance.

Next Wednesday at 7 PM, we'll host a children's storytelling event for ages 4-8. Our staff will read interactive stories and lead simple craft activities. There's no need to register for this event, but parents must accompany their children.

questions.stem: When is Helen Graham's reading?
questions.explanation: 「This Saturday, we have author Helen Graham reading from her new novel ... reading begins at 3 PM」が根拠。

questions.stem: What is the children's event age range?
questions.explanation: 「children's storytelling event for ages 4-8」が根拠。

question_data.audio_script:
[IELTS Listening Section 4 - Academic Lecture: The Origins of Writing]

Today's lecture explores the origins of writing — one of humanity's most consequential inventions. Writing emerged independently in at least three locations: Mesopotamia around 3200 BCE, Egypt around 3100 BCE, and China around 1300 BCE. Some scholars argue that Mesoamerican writing systems also developed independently around 600 BCE.

The earliest writing was primarily practical — keeping records of grain storage, livestock counts, and tax payments. Only later did writing expand into literature, philosophy, and historical record. The development from accounting tools to literary expression took centuries in each tradition.

questions.stem: Where did writing first emerge around 3200 BCE?
questions.explanation: 「Mesopotamia around 3200 BCE」が根拠。

questions.stem: What was the earliest writing primarily used for?
choices: Practical records: grain storage, livestock, tax payments
questions.explanation: 「earliest writing was primarily practical — keeping records of grain storage, livestock counts, and tax payments」が根拠。

question_data.passage:
The Promise and Limits of Telemedicine

Telemedicine — the delivery of medical care through video consultations and digital communication — has expanded dramatically in recent years, accelerated particularly by the COVID-19 pandemic. The expansion has produced both genuine benefits and revealed important limitations of remote medical care.

The benefits are substantial. Patients in rural areas gain access to specialists previously unavailable locally. Routine follow-up visits become more convenient when patients need not take time off work. Mental health services, which often involve primarily conversation, translate especially well to remote delivery. The cost savings for both patients and healthcare systems can be significant.

Yet substantial limitations have become clear. Physical examinations remain difficult or impossible through video. Conditions where touch, smell, or detailed visual inspection matter cannot be adequately assessed remotely. The relationship-building that good medicine often requires happens differently — sometimes less effectively — through screens. Patients without reliable internet access face new forms of healthcare inequality. Telemedicine works best as a complement to in-person care rather than a replacement.

questions.stem: What benefits of telemedicine does the author identify?
choices: Rural specialist access, convenient follow-ups, mental health translation, cost savings
questions.explanation: 段落2「Patients in rural areas gain access to specialists ... Routine follow-up visits become more convenient ... Mental health services ... cost savings」が根拠。

questions.stem: What limitations does the author identify?
choices: Difficult physical exams, conditions requiring touch/smell, weaker relationship-building, internet access inequality
questions.explanation: 段落3「Physical examinations remain difficult ... touch, smell, or detailed visual inspection ... relationship-building ... internet access」が根拠。

question_data.passage:
The Philosophy of Time

The nature of time has puzzled philosophers since antiquity. Common sense suggests time flows inevitably from past to future, with the present moment existing as a kind of moving boundary. Yet careful philosophical and scientific analysis has revealed this picture to be substantially more problematic than it appears.

A fundamental question concerns whether the past and future have the same kind of reality as the present. The 'presentist' position holds that only the present moment is real — the past has happened but no longer exists, and the future has not yet come into being. The 'eternalist' position, by contrast, holds that all moments — past, present, and future — exist equally, with the appearance of temporal flow being a feature of how conscious beings experience reality rather than an objective feature of time itself.

Developments in physics complicate the matter further. Einstein's theory of relativity demonstrates that simultaneity is not absolute — different observers in different reference frames disagree about whether two events occur at the same time. This finding suggests that the concept of a universal 'now' may be incoherent, supporting eternalist over presentist positions.

The philosophical implications are substantial. If all moments exist equally, then in some sense everything that ever happens is permanently real — the past is not lost but only temporally distant from our present perspective. The implications for meaning, mortality, and the significance of present action remain matters of ongoing debate.

questions.stem: What is the 'eternalist' position?
choices: All moments — past, present, future — exist equally; flow is a feature of conscious experience
questions.explanation: 段落2「The 'eternalist' position ... holds that all moments — past, present, and future — exist equally, with the appearance of temporal flow being a feature of how conscious beings experience reality」が根拠。

questions.stem: How does relativity complicate the matter?
choices: Shows simultaneity is not absolute — universal 'now' may be incoherent, supporting eternalism
questions.explanation: 段落3「simultaneity is not absolute ... universal 'now' may be incoherent, supporting eternalist over presentist positions」が根拠。

question_data.prompt:
[IELTS Writing Task 1 — 150 words minimum, 20 min]

The pie charts below show the proportion of household expenditure on different categories in Japan in 2000 and 2024.

2000:
- Food: 25%
- Housing: 22%
- Transport: 14%
- Education: 10%
- Healthcare: 6%
- Communication: 4%
- Other: 19%

2024:
- Food: 22%
- Housing: 25%
- Transport: 11%
- Education: 8%
- Healthcare: 11%
- Communication: 9%
- Other: 14%

Summarize the information by selecting and reporting the main features.
question_data.sample:
The pie charts illustrate how Japanese household expenditure was distributed across seven categories in 2000 and 2024.

Overall, while basic categories like food and housing remained the largest expenditure areas across both periods, several notable shifts occurred. Healthcare and communication costs nearly doubled in their share of household budgets, while education, transport, and 'other' categories declined.

In 2000, food represented the largest expenditure at 25%, followed by housing at 22%. Transport accounted for 14%, with education at 10% and healthcare at just 6%. Communication formed only 4% of household spending.

By 2024, the picture had shifted noticeably. Housing became the largest category at 25%, surpassing food, which dropped slightly to 22%. The most striking changes occurred in healthcare and communication. Healthcare nearly doubled from 6% to 11%, reflecting Japan's aging population. Communication more than doubled from 4% to 9%, reflecting the spread of mobile devices and internet services.

Meanwhile, transport, education, and 'other' categories all declined, freeing budget space for the increased healthcare and communication costs. (167 words)

questions.stem: Above prompt — IELTS Task 1 (150+ words)
questions.explanation: 【IELTS Task 1・pie chart】① 概要 ② Overall trend (主要 vs 縮小) ③ 2000年詳述 ④ 2024年変化 ⑤ 因果説明 (高齢化、デジタル化)。

questions.stem: 【共通テスト型・化学基礎】次の中で原子量が最も大きいものはどれか。原子番号が異なるが質量数が同じものを 'isobar (同重体)' と呼ぶ。
questions.explanation: 【共通テスト・化学基礎】よく出題される原子量を覚える: H=1, C=12, N=14, O=16, Na=23, Mg=24, Al=27, S=32, Cl=35.5, K=39, Ca=40, Fe=56。

questions.stem: 【共通テスト型】1.0 mol/L の希塩酸 100 mL に含まれる H+ の物質量を求めよ。
【共通テスト型・化学基礎】次のうちアルカリ金属はどれか。Li, Be, B, C, N。
questions.explanation: アルカリ金属 = 周期表 1 族 (H 除く): Li, Na, K, Rb, Cs, Fr。Be はアルカリ土類金属、B-N は非金属または半金属。

questions.stem: 【共通テスト型】水 H2O 18 g を電気分解したとき、生成する水素 H2 と酸素 O2 の物質量を求めよ。原子量 H=1, O=16。
【共通テスト型・数IA】サイコロを2回振って出る目の和が 7 になる確率を求めよ。
【共通テスト型】二次関数 y = x² - 4x + 3 のグラフが x 軸と交わる x 座標を求めよ。
【共通テスト型・数IA】sin² θ + cos² θ の値を求めよ。
questions.explanation: 三角関数の基本恒等式。

questions.stem: 【共通テスト型】1 から 100 までの自然数で、3 の倍数は何個あるか。
【共通テスト型・数IIB】等差数列 1, 4, 7, 10, ... の第10項を求めよ。
【共通テスト型】log₂ 8 + log₂ 4 を計算せよ。
【共通テスト型・数IIB】sin(π/6) + cos(π/3) の値を求めよ。
【共通テスト型】導関数の定義により f(x) = x² の導関数を求めよ。
questions.explanation: f'(x) = lim_{h→0} ((x+h)² - x²)/h = lim_{h→0} (2xh + h²)/h = lim_{h→0} (2x + h) = 2x。

questions.stem: 【共通テスト型・物理基礎】質量 2.0 kg の物体を高さ 5.0 m まで持ち上げたときの位置エネルギーの増加を求めよ。g = 9.8 m/s²。
questions.explanation: 位置エネルギー U = mgh の公式。

questions.stem: 【共通テスト型】100 V の電源に 20 Ω の抵抗を接続したときの電流を求めよ。
questions.explanation: オームの法則 V = IR → I = V/R。

questions.stem: 【阪大型】関数 f(x) = x³ - 3x² + 2 の極値を求めよ。
【阪大型】定積分 ∫₀² (3x² - 2x + 1) dx を求めよ。
questions.explanation: ∫(3x² - 2x + 1)dx = x³ - x² + x。[x³-x²+x]₀² = 8-4+2 = 6。

questions.stem: 【東工大型】複素数 z = 1 + i の極形式を求めよ (絶対値と偏角)。
questions.explanation: |z| = √(1²+1²) = √2。tan θ = 1/1 = 1 → θ = π/4 (第一象限)。極形式: r(cos θ + i sin θ)。

questions.stem: 【東工大型】lim_{n→∞} (1 + 1/n)^n の値を求めよ。
questions.explanation: 自然対数の底 e の定義式。

questions.stem: 【名大型】行列 A = ((1,2),(3,4)) の行列式 |A| を求めよ。
questions.explanation: 2×2 行列の行列式 = ad - bc。

questions.stem: 【名大型】自然数 n の階乗 n! が 100 の倍数となる最小の n を求めよ。
questions.explanation: 100 = 4·25 = 2²·5²。階乗中の 5 の個数: n=5 で 1個、n=10 で 2個。2 の個数は十分。よって n=10 が初めて 5² を含む → 10! = 3628800 (確かに 100 の倍数)。

questions.stem: 【早稲田理工型】関数 f(x) = ln(x² + 1) の x = 0 における2階微分 f''(0) を求めよ。
【早稲田理工型】数列 a_n = (1/2)^n の無限和 Σ_{n=1}^∞ a_n を求めよ。
【上智理工型】tan(π/4) + tan(π/3) の値を求めよ。
【上智理工型】三角不等式: 全ての実数 a, b について |a + b| ≤ |a| + |b| を示せ。
questions.answer: 両辺二乗: (a+b)² = a² + 2ab + b² ≤ a² + 2|ab| + b² = (|a|+|b|)²。よって |a+b| ≤ |a|+|b|。等号成立は ab≥0 のとき。
questions.explanation: 絶対値の基本性質。任意の実数 ab について ab ≤ |ab|。

questions.stem: 【国公立医学部・生物】タンパク質合成における転写と翻訳について、転写と翻訳がそれぞれ細胞のどこで行われるか述べよ (真核生物)。
questions.answer: 転写は核内で行われ、翻訳は細胞質のリボソームで行われる。
questions.explanation: 【国公立医学部型】真核生物では転写 (DNA → mRNA) は核内、その後 mRNA は核外へ移送され、細胞質のリボソームで翻訳 (mRNA → タンパク質) される。原核生物では両方が細胞質で同時進行。

questions.stem: 【国公立医学部・生物】免疫系における B 細胞と T 細胞のそれぞれの主な役割を簡潔に述べよ。
questions.answer: B 細胞は抗体を産生して液性免疫を担う。T 細胞は細胞性免疫を担い、ヘルパー T 細胞 (B 細胞の活性化) とキラー T 細胞 (感染細胞の破壊) に分化する。
questions.explanation: 【国公立医学部型】獲得免疫の基本。B 細胞 = 体液中で抗体産生、T 細胞 = 細胞同士の直接相互作用で感染細胞や腫瘍細胞を排除。両者は協調して感染防御を行う。

questions.stem: This ( ) my pen.
questions.explanation: 単数 This → is。

questions.stem: I usually ( ) breakfast at 7.
questions.explanation: 主語 I + 現在の習慣 → have (原形)。

questions.stem: Where ( ) my keys?
questions.explanation: 複数 my keys → are。

questions.stem: My brother ( ) soccer.
questions.explanation: 3単現 My brother → likes。

questions.stem: I ( ) up at 7 every morning.
questions.explanation: 主語 I + 現在の習慣 → get。

questions.stem: That ( ) a beautiful flower.
questions.explanation: 単数 That → is。

questions.stem: My sister ( ) breakfast every morning.
questions.explanation: 3単現 My sister → cooks。

questions.stem: There ( ) a cat under the table.
questions.explanation: 単数 a cat → is。

questions.stem: How ( ) do you study every day?
questions.explanation: 時間の長さ → How long (study time)。

questions.stem: I want ( ) a doctor in the future.
questions.explanation: want + to 不定詞。

questions.stem: Mike has ( ) than Yumi.
questions.explanation: than → 比較級。more books = より多くの本。

questions.stem: It is fun ( ) baseball.
questions.explanation: It is + 形容詞 + to 不定詞。

questions.stem: The court ( ) the original ruling after careful review.
questions.explanation: overturn a ruling = 判決を覆す。overlook = 見落とす、overhear = 漏れ聞く、overflow = 溢れる。

questions.stem: His behavior was ( ) inappropriate for the formal event.
questions.explanation: distinctly inappropriate = 明らかに不適切。distantly = 遠く、distractedly = 上の空で、disturbingly = 不穏に。

questions.stem: The committee ( ) all relevant data before reaching a decision.
questions.explanation: scrutinize = 精査する。scintillate = きらめく、scuttle = 急ぐ、scramble = よじ登る。

questions.stem: The findings have ( ) implications for current treatment protocols.
questions.explanation: profound implications = 深遠な含意。profane = 不敬な、prolific = 多産の、profligate = 浪費的な。

questions.stem: The economist's predictions proved ( ) accurate over time.
questions.explanation: remarkably accurate = 驚くほど正確。reluctantly = 気が進まず、resentfully = 憤って。

questions.stem: The new technology has ( ) the way we communicate.
questions.explanation: revolutionize = 革命を起こす。reverberate = 反響、reconcile = 和解、relinquish = 放棄。

questions.stem: The students were ( ) for their hard work.
questions.explanation: were + 過去分詞 (受動態) → rewarded。

questions.stem: He prefers tea ( ) coffee.
questions.explanation: prefer A to B = B より A を好む。

questions.stem: The meeting will be held ( ) tomorrow.
questions.explanation: 時刻の前 → at。

question_data.prompt:
TOPIC: Should everyone learn how to cook?

POINTS to consider (use 2): - Health - Money - Time - Family

Requirements: 50-60 words.
question_data.sample:
I think everyone should learn how to cook. I have two reasons.

First, cooking helps with health. Home-cooked meals usually have less salt, sugar, and oil than restaurant food. Second, cooking saves money. Eating out every day is expensive, but cooking at home is cheap.

For these reasons, cooking is an important skill. (54 words)

questions.stem: Above prompt — 50-60 word opinion essay.
questions.explanation: 【2級】Health + Money の 2 points。

question_data.prompt:
TOPIC: Should countries spend more on space exploration?

POINTS to consider (use 2 of 4): - Science - Cost - Inspiration - Earth's problems

Requirements: 120-150 words.
question_data.sample:
I support increased spending on space exploration. Two arguments justify this position: scientific advancement and inspirational impact.

First, space exploration drives scientific progress with broad applications. Technologies developed for space missions — GPS, satellite weather forecasting, water purification, advanced materials — have transformed daily life on Earth. The economic returns from space-derived technologies far exceed the original investments.

Second, space programs inspire generations of scientists and engineers. The Apollo missions motivated countless young people to pursue STEM careers, addressing critical workforce shortages. Mars rovers and the James Webb Space Telescope continue this inspiration today, pulling students into fields where they will tackle Earth's most pressing problems.

While critics emphasize Earth's problems, abandoning space exploration would not solve them. The same scientific capacity that enables space programs is needed to address climate change, energy challenges, and disease. (140 words)

questions.stem: Above prompt — 120-150 word essay.
questions.explanation: 【準1級】Science + Inspiration の 2 points・spinoff 論証・反論への組込。

question_data.passage:
The Microbiome Revolution

In recent decades, scientific understanding of the microbiome — the trillions of microorganisms that inhabit human bodies — has undergone dramatic transformation. Once viewed primarily as a source of disease, gut bacteria are now recognized as essential partners in human health, influencing digestion, immunity, mental health, and even susceptibility to chronic diseases.

The scale of this microbial ecosystem is striking. The average adult harbors approximately 38 trillion bacteria, slightly outnumbering human cells. These microbes collectively contain millions of genes, far more than the human genome itself. The diversity of species varies substantially among individuals, shaped by genetics, diet, environment, and antibiotic exposure.

Research has revealed remarkable connections between microbiome composition and health outcomes. Specific bacterial profiles correlate with obesity, autoimmune diseases, and even depression. Antibiotic use, while life-saving, can disrupt microbiome balance with consequences that may persist for years. The 'hygiene hypothesis' — that excessive cleanliness in childhood may impair immune development by limiting microbial exposure — has gained empirical support.

Clinical applications are emerging. Fecal microbiota transplants successfully treat certain difficult intestinal infections. Probiotic and prebiotic interventions are being studied for various conditions. Personalized nutrition recommendations based on individual microbiome profiles represent an emerging field.

Yet much remains unknown. The complexity of these microbial ecosystems exceeds current analytical capabilities. Causation versus correlation often remains unclear. The clinical promise of microbiome-based therapies has yet to fully materialize despite substantial research investment.

questions.stem: What is the scale of the human microbiome?
choices: Approximately 38 trillion bacteria — slightly outnumbering human cells
questions.explanation: 段落2「approximately 38 trillion bacteria, slightly outnumbering human cells」が根拠。

questions.stem: What conditions are linked to microbiome composition?
questions.explanation: 段落3「Specific bacterial profiles correlate with obesity, autoimmune diseases, and even depression」が根拠。

questions.stem: What clinical applications are emerging?
choices: Fecal microbiota transplants, probiotic/prebiotic interventions, personalized nutrition based on microbiome profiles
questions.explanation: 段落4「Fecal microbiota transplants ... Probiotic and prebiotic interventions ... Personalized nutrition recommendations」が根拠。

question_data.passage:
The Sharing Economy Reconsidered

The sharing economy — companies enabling peer-to-peer rental of goods and services — emerged in the early 2010s with promises of more efficient resource use, expanded economic opportunities, and reduced environmental impact. A decade later, the actual record is more complicated than initial enthusiasm suggested.

The success stories are real. Ride-sharing services have provided convenient transportation in many cities and additional income for drivers. Short-term rental platforms enabled property owners to monetize unused space and travelers to access lodging at competitive prices. Tool libraries, clothing rentals, and other peer-to-peer services have allowed people to access goods without ownership.

Yet substantial concerns have emerged. Many sharing economy workers face conditions that traditional employment regulations were designed to prevent: unpredictable income, lack of benefits, and limited bargaining power. Cities have grappled with the impacts of short-term rental platforms on housing affordability, with rentals removing units from long-term housing markets and raising prices for residents. The environmental benefits, once assumed, have proven complicated as platforms generate new traffic and travel patterns.

Responses to these issues have been uneven. Some jurisdictions have imposed regulations requiring sharing economy platforms to comply with existing rules — taxi licensing, hotel regulations, employment standards. Others have allowed largely free operation. The varying approaches have produced different outcomes that researchers are now beginning to evaluate.

Looking forward, the question is not whether the sharing economy will continue but in what form. The technology and consumer demand exist; the policy framework that determines how the model operates remains contested. Decisions made by regulators, workers, consumers, and platforms themselves will shape whether the next decade brings genuinely valuable innovation or the entrenchment of problematic practices.

questions.stem: What success stories does the author identify?
choices: Ride-sharing convenience/income, short-term rental income, tool/clothing rental access without ownership
questions.explanation: 段落2「Ride-sharing services have provided convenient transportation ... Short-term rental platforms enabled property owners to monetize ... Tool libraries, clothing rentals」が根拠。

questions.stem: What concerns have emerged?
choices: Worker conditions (unpredictable income, no benefits), housing affordability impacts, complicated environmental record
questions.explanation: 段落3「sharing economy workers face conditions ... unpredictable income, lack of benefits ... housing affordability ... environmental benefits ... complicated」が根拠。

question_data.prompt:
[TOEFL Speaking Task 1 — Independent Speaking]

Do you agree or disagree with the following statement? Children should be required to read for at least one hour each day. Use specific reasons and examples to support your answer.
question_data.sample: I disagree with mandatory daily reading requirements. While reading is valuable, mandates often produce counterproductive results. First, forcing children to read often kills the genuine love of reading we want them to develop. Mandatory reading becomes a chore associated with obligation rather than pleasure. Second, rigid time requirements work against the diverse ways children develop reading interest. Some children develop intense reading interest at age 6, others at age 12; rigid daily mandates harm both groups. Better approaches expose children to many books, model reading by adults, and let intrinsic interest develop. The result is typically more lifetime reading than mandates produce. (105 words)

questions.stem: Above prompt — 45 second response.
questions.explanation: 【TOEFL Speaking Task 1】Disagree position + 2 reasons (内発的動機 / 多様な発達ペース) + 代替案。

question_data.prompt:
[TOEFL Speaking Task 1 — Independent Speaking]

Some people believe it's better to learn from older, experienced people. Others say it's better to learn from people their own age. Which view do you support?
question_data.sample: I think learning from both groups serves different purposes. Older, experienced people offer perspective that comes only with years — patterns observed across decades, mistakes already made and avoided, wisdom about what matters in the long run. Their guidance is invaluable for major life decisions and complex strategic thinking. Same-age peers offer different value — relevance to current technologies and cultural contexts, shared challenges, immediate empathy. They often understand specific contemporary problems older people may dismiss as trivial. The optimal approach combines both: learn enduring principles from experienced mentors, learn current applications from peers, and recognize that each source illuminates what the other cannot. (105 words)

questions.stem: Above prompt — 45 second response.
questions.explanation: 【TOEFL Speaking Task 1】Both/and 立場 + 各々の独自価値 + Synthesis 結論。
